contents.value:
```ini
[Unit]
Description=My Web Application
Documentation=https://docs.example.com

# Dependencies (what to start):
Requires=postgresql.service     # Hard: fail if postgres fails
Wants=redis.service             # Soft: ok if redis fails
BindsTo=docker.service          # Stop if docker stops

# Ordering (when to start):
After=network-online.target postgresql.service
Before=nginx.service

# Conditions:
ConditionPathExists=/etc/myapp/config.yml
ConditionFileNotEmpty=/etc/myapp/license.key

# On failure:
OnFailure=notify-admin@%n.service
```
**[Unit]** defines metadata and dependencies. Ordering (After/Before) and requirements (Requires/Wants) are independent — use both for correct startup order.

```ini
[Service]
# Startup types:
# simple  - process IS the service (default)
# exec    - like simple, started after exec()
# forking - traditional daemon (fork+exit parent)
# oneshot - run and exit (scripts)
# notify  - sends sd_notify() when ready
# dbus    - ready when D-Bus name acquired
Type=simple

User=appuser
Group=appuser
WorkingDirectory=/opt/app

ExecStartPre=-/opt/app/pre-check.sh  # - = ignore failure
ExecStart=/opt/app/bin/server
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID

Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StartLimitBurst=3
StartLimitIntervalSec=60

Environment=NODE_ENV=production
EnvironmentFile=-/opt/app/.env

StandardOutput=journal
SyslogIdentifier=myapp
```
**[Service]** configures how the service starts, stops, and restarts. Choose Type= carefully — it affects how systemd determines readiness.

```ini
# Type=simple (default, most common)
# Process started by ExecStart IS the main process
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node server.js

# Type=exec (like simple, but started after exec())
# More reliable startup detection
Type=exec

# Type=forking (traditional daemons)
# Parent forks, exits; child is the daemon
Type=forking
PIDFile=/var/run/myapp.pid
ExecStart=/usr/sbin/mydaemon -d

# Type=notify (modern services)
# Service calls sd_notify(READY=1) when ready
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=main

# Type=oneshot (scripts, setup tasks)
# Runs to completion, then considered started
Type=oneshot
RemainAfterExit=yes
ExecStart=/opt/scripts/setup.sh
```
**Type** determines how systemd considers the service 'started'. Use `simple` for foreground processes, `forking` for traditional daemons, `notify` for sd_notify()-capable services, `oneshot` for scripts.

```ini
# Restart conditions:
# no          - never restart (default)
# always      - always restart
# on-failure  - non-zero exit, signal, timeout, watchdog
# on-abnormal - signal, timeout, watchdog (not clean non-zero)
# on-abort    - unclean signal only
# on-watchdog - watchdog timeout only
# on-success  - clean exit (exit 0) only

Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5                    # Wait 5s before restart

# Rate limiting:
StartLimitBurst=5               # Max 5 restarts...
StartLimitIntervalSec=60        # ...in 60 seconds
# After limit: unit enters 'failed' state

# What counts as failure:
# Exit code != 0, killed by signal, core dump,
# timeout, watchdog timeout

# Override exit codes:
SuccessExitStatus=143           # Treat SIGTERM exit as success
RestartPreventExitStatus=1      # Don't restart on exit 1
RestartForceExitStatus=SIGABRT  # Always restart on SIGABRT
```
**Restart** policy determines when the service is automatically restarted. `on-failure` is most common. Combine with `RestartSec` and `StartLimitBurst` to prevent restart loops.

```ini
# Calendar expressions:
OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:00:00       # Daily at 2 AM
OnCalendar=Mon *-*-* 09:00:00   # Every Monday 9 AM
OnCalendar=*-*-01 00:00:00      # 1st of each month
OnCalendar=*:0/15               # Every 15 minutes
OnCalendar=hourly               # Every hour
OnCalendar=daily                # Every day at midnight
OnCalendar=weekly               # Every Monday midnight
OnCalendar=monthly              # 1st of month midnight

# Monotonic timers:
OnBootSec=5min                  # 5 min after boot
OnUnitActiveSec=15min           # Every 15 min (repeating)
OnStartupSec=30s                # 30s after systemd starts

# Options:
Persistent=true                 # Run missed events
RandomizedDelaySec=300          # Random 0-5min delay
AccuracySec=1s                  # Precision (default: 1min)

# Test expressions:
# systemd-analyze calendar '*-*-* 02:00:00'
# systemd-analyze calendar 'Mon *-*-* 09:00:00' --iterations=5
```
**Timers** replace cron with more features: persistent (catch-up after downtime), randomized delay (prevent thundering herd), and monotonic schedules. Use `systemd-analyze calendar` to test expressions.

```ini
[Install]
# Most common — start at boot:
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# For graphical login:
WantedBy=graphical.target

# For timer units:
WantedBy=timers.target

# For socket units:
WantedBy=sockets.target

# Enable companion units together:
Also=myapp.socket
Also=myapp.timer

# How it works:
# 'systemctl enable myapp.service' creates a symlink:
# /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/myapp.service
#   -> /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service
#
# This makes the target 'want' the service,
# so it starts automatically at boot.
```
**[Install]** tells systemd what happens when you `systemctl enable` a unit. `WantedBy=multi-user.target` means 'start at boot'. This section is only used by enable/disable.

```ini
# Progressive security hardening:

# Level 1: Basic isolation
NoNewPrivileges=true
PrivateTmp=true
ProtectHome=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ReadWritePaths=/opt/app/data

# Level 2: + Kernel protection
ProtectKernelTunables=true
ProtectKernelModules=true
ProtectKernelLogs=true
ProtectControlGroups=true
ProtectHostname=true
ProtectClock=true

# Level 3: + Capability dropping
CapabilityBoundingSet=
# CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE for port < 1024
AmbientCapabilities=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE

# Level 4: + Syscall filtering
SystemCallFilter=@system-service
SystemCallErrorNumber=EPERM
SystemCallArchitectures=native

# Level 5: + Network restrictions
RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_UNIX
RestrictNamespaces=true
LockPersonality=true
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=true

# Audit your security:
# systemd-analyze security myapp.service
```
**Security hardening** is progressive — start with basic isolation and add more restrictions. Use `systemd-analyze security` to audit your service. Score of 0-2 = good.

```ini
# myapp.socket — triggers myapp.service on connection
[Unit]
Description=MyApp Socket

[Socket]
# TCP port:
ListenStream=8080

# Unix socket:
ListenStream=/run/myapp.sock
SocketUser=www-data
SocketGroup=www-data
SocketMode=0660

# UDP:
ListenDatagram=514

# Options:
Accept=no          # no: one service for all (default)
                   # yes: one service per connection
Backlog=128
MaxConnections=256
KeepAlive=true
NoDelay=true
FreeBind=true      # Bind even if address not yet available

[Install]
WantedBy=sockets.target

# Benefits:
# - Service starts on first connection (lazy loading)
# - Zero-downtime restarts (socket stays open)
# - systemd handles socket permissions
```
**Socket activation** lets systemd manage the listening socket. The service starts on first connection, enabling lazy loading and zero-downtime restarts.

```ini
# Template unit: myapp@.service (note the @)
# Run multiple instances with different parameters

[Unit]
Description=MyApp on port %i
After=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=appuser
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/bin/server --port %i
Restart=on-failure

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# Usage:
# systemctl start myapp@8080.service
# systemctl start myapp@8081.service
# systemctl enable myapp@8080.service

# Specifiers in templates:
# %i  Instance name (e.g., 8080)
# %I  Unescaped instance name
# %p  Prefix (e.g., myapp)
# %n  Full name (e.g., myapp@8080.service)

# List instances:
# systemctl list-units 'myapp@*'
```
**Template units** (name@.service) create parameterized services. The instance name after @ is available as %i. Great for running multiple copies on different ports, configs, or users.

```bash
# View logs for a service:
journalctl -u myapp.service

# Follow (tail) logs:
journalctl -u myapp.service -f

# Since last boot:
journalctl -u myapp.service -b

# Last 100 lines:
journalctl -u myapp.service -n 100

# Time range:
journalctl -u myapp --since '2024-01-01' --until '2024-01-02'
journalctl -u myapp --since '1 hour ago'

# Priority filter:
journalctl -u myapp -p err        # Errors and above
journalctl -u myapp -p warning     # Warnings and above

# Output formats:
journalctl -u myapp -o json        # JSON output
journalctl -u myapp -o json-pretty # Pretty JSON
journalctl -u myapp -o short-iso   # ISO timestamps

# Disk usage:
journalctl --disk-usage
journalctl --vacuum-size=500M      # Reduce to 500MB
journalctl --vacuum-time=30d       # Keep 30 days
```
**journalctl** reads the systemd journal. Use `-u` for service logs, `-f` to follow, `-p` for priority filtering. JSON output is useful for log aggregation.

```bash
# Common targets:
# multi-user.target  - Normal multi-user system (most services)
# graphical.target   - GUI environment
# network.target     - Network stack is up
# network-online.target - Network is fully configured (use this!)
# timers.target      - Timer units auto-start
# sockets.target     - Socket units auto-start
# basic.target       - Basic system initialization
# sysinit.target     - System initialization
# rescue.target      - Single-user rescue mode
# emergency.target   - Emergency shell
# shutdown.target    - System is shutting down
# reboot.target      - System is rebooting
# poweroff.target    - System is powering off

# Check default target:
systemctl get-default

# Set default:
systemctl set-default multi-user.target

# Switch target now:
systemctl isolate rescue.target

# List all targets:
systemctl list-units --type=target
```
**Targets** are systemd's equivalent of run levels. Most services use `WantedBy=multi-user.target` (boot). Use `network-online.target` (not network.target) for services needing network.

```ini
# Single variable:
Environment=NODE_ENV=production

# Multiple on one line (space-separated):
Environment=NODE_ENV=production PORT=3000 LOG_LEVEL=info

# Multiple lines:
Environment=NODE_ENV=production
Environment=PORT=3000
Environment=DATABASE_URL=postgres://...

# From file (KEY=VALUE format):
EnvironmentFile=/opt/app/.env
EnvironmentFile=-/opt/app/.env.local  # - = ignore if missing

# .env file format:
# NODE_ENV=production
# PORT=3000
# # Comments allowed
# DATABASE_URL="postgres://user:pass@host/db"

# Override per-instance (drop-in):
# systemctl edit myapp.service
# Creates: /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service.d/override.conf
# [Service]
# Environment=DEBUG=true
```
**Environment** variables can be set inline, from files, or via drop-in overrides. File format is simple KEY=VALUE. Use `-` prefix on EnvironmentFile to ignore missing files.
A **section** groups related configuration entries under a named header.
A **key** (or property name) identifies a configuration setting.
A **value** is the data assigned to a configuration key.
A **comment** annotates configuration without affecting behavior. Usually starts with `#` or `;`.
A **string value** contains text, optionally quoted with single or double quotes.
A **boolean value** represents true/false, on/off, yes/no.
An **integer value** is a whole number used for ports, counts, sizes.
An **array** stores multiple values for a single key.
An **include** directive loads configuration from another file.
A **variable** references another value using interpolation syntax.
A **block** groups nested configuration entries.
A **directive** is a configuration command that controls behavior.
A **default value** is used when no explicit value is provided.
An **override** replaces a previously defined setting with a new value.

```systemd
[Unit]
```
Generic unit configuration. Defines dependencies, ordering, conditions. Present in all unit types.

```systemd
[Service]
```
Service-specific configuration: startup type, exec commands, restart policy, resource limits, security sandbox.

```systemd
[Install]
```
Installation info for systemctl enable/disable. Defines which target pulls this unit.

```systemd
[Timer]
```
Timer configuration. Calendar or monotonic schedules. Replaces cron with more features.

```systemd
[Socket]
```
Socket activation. Systemd listens, starts service on connection. Enables zero-downtime restarts.

```systemd
[Path]
```
Path-based activation. Watches filesystem paths and starts a unit when conditions are met.

```systemd
[Mount]
```
Mount point configuration. Equivalent to fstab entries but managed by systemd.

```systemd
[Swap]
```
Swap device/file configuration.

```systemd
[Slice]
```
Resource control configuration for a group of units using cgroup hierarchy.

```systemd
Description=string
```
Human-readable description of the unit. Shown in status output and logs.

```systemd
Documentation=URL [URL ...]
```
URIs pointing to documentation. Supports http://, https://, file:, info:, man: schemes.

```systemd
After=unit [unit ...]
```
Ordering dependency — start this unit after the listed units. Does NOT create a requirement, only ordering.

```systemd
Before=unit [unit ...]
```
Start this unit before the listed units.

```systemd
Requires=unit [unit ...]
```
Hard requirement. If any required unit fails to start, this unit is also not started. Use with After= for ordering.

```systemd
Wants=unit [unit ...]
```
Soft requirement. Like Requires but this unit still starts even if wanted unit fails. Preferred over Requires.

```systemd
BindsTo=unit [unit ...]
```
Stronger than Requires: this unit is stopped when the bound unit is stopped (not just when it fails to start).

```systemd
PartOf=unit [unit ...]
```
When the listed unit is stopped or restarted, this unit is also stopped/restarted. Does not affect starting.

```systemd
Conflicts=unit [unit ...]
```
Negative dependency. Starting this unit stops conflicting units and vice versa.

```systemd
ConditionPathExists=[!]path
```
Check if path exists before starting. Prefix with ! to negate. Prefix with - to make non-fatal.

```systemd
ConditionFileNotEmpty=[!]path
```
Check if file exists and is not empty.

```systemd
ConditionDirectoryNotEmpty=[!]path
```
Check if directory exists and is not empty.

```systemd
AssertPathExists=[!]path
```
Like ConditionPathExists but causes a failure (not skip) if not met.

```systemd
OnFailure=unit [unit ...]
```
Units to activate when this unit enters a 'failed' state. Useful for alerting.

```systemd
OnSuccess=unit [unit ...]
```
Units to activate when this unit succeeds (deactivates cleanly).

```systemd
ExecStart=command
```
Main command to start the service. Must be an absolute path. Use $MAINPID for the main process PID.

```systemd
ExecStartPre=[-]command
```
Commands to run before ExecStart. Prefix with '-' to ignore failure. Prefix with '+' to run as root.

```systemd
ExecStartPost=[-]command
```
Commands to run after ExecStart. Runs after the service is considered 'started'.

```systemd
ExecStop=command
```
Command to stop the service. If not set, SIGTERM is sent. $MAINPID holds the main process PID.

```systemd
ExecStopPost=[-]command
```
Commands to run after the service is stopped. Runs even on failure.

```systemd
ExecReload=command
```
Command to reload configuration. Commonly: /bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID.

```systemd
RestartSec=time
```
Time to wait before restarting. Default: 100ms. Accepts s, min, h, ms, us units.

```systemd
StartLimitBurst=n
```
Maximum number of starts within StartLimitIntervalSec before the unit is held back. Default: 5.

```systemd
StartLimitIntervalSec=time
```
Time window for start rate limiting. Default: 10s. Set to 0 to disable.

```systemd
TimeoutStartSec=time
```
Timeout for service to start. If exceeded, service is killed (SIGTERM then SIGKILL). Default: 90s.

```systemd
TimeoutStopSec=time
```
Timeout for service to stop. After this, SIGKILL is sent. Default: 90s.

```systemd
WatchdogSec=time
```
Watchdog timeout. Service must call sd_notify(WATCHDOG=1) within this interval or be restarted.

```systemd
KillMode=control-group|mixed|process|none
```
How to kill processes: control-group (all in cgroup), mixed (SIGTERM main, SIGKILL rest), process (main only).

```systemd
KillSignal=signal
```
Signal to send when stopping. Default: SIGTERM. Also: SIGINT, SIGQUIT, etc.

```systemd
User=username
```
Run the service as this user. Creates a user namespace if DynamicUser=yes.

```systemd
Group=groupname
```
Run the service with this group.

```systemd
DynamicUser=yes|no
```
Allocate a dynamic user/group at runtime. Automatically enables other sandboxing options.

```systemd
WorkingDirectory=path
```
Set working directory before starting. Special: ~ for user home, - prefix to ignore if missing.

```systemd
RootDirectory=path
```
Set root directory (chroot) for the service.

```systemd
Environment=KEY=value [KEY2=value2 ...]
```
Set environment variables. For multiple: use multiple Environment= lines or EnvironmentFile=.

```systemd
EnvironmentFile=[-]path
```
Read environment variables from file (KEY=value format). Prefix with - to ignore if missing.

```systemd
StandardOutput=inherit|null|tty|journal|kmsg|socket|file:path|append:path
```
Where to send stdout. journal: systemd journal. file:path: write to file. append:path: append.

```systemd
StandardError=inherit|null|tty|journal|kmsg|socket|file:path|append:path
```
Where to send stderr. inherit: same as StandardOutput.

```systemd
SyslogIdentifier=string
```
Identifier for syslog/journal entries. Defaults to process name. Used with journalctl -u.

```systemd
PIDFile=path
```
Path to PID file. Required for Type=forking so systemd can track the main process.

```systemd
RemainAfterExit=yes|no
```
Service remains active after main process exits. Essential for Type=oneshot.

```systemd
NotifyAccess=none|main|exec|all
```
Which processes can send sd_notify() messages. Required for Type=notify.

```systemd
LimitNOFILE=[soft:]hard
```
Maximum open file descriptors. Commonly set to 65535 for servers. Maps to ulimit -n.

```systemd
LimitNPROC=[soft:]hard
```
Maximum number of processes for the user. Maps to ulimit -u.

```systemd
LimitCORE=[soft:]hard
```
Core dump file size. Set to infinity for debugging, 0 for production.

```systemd
LimitMEMLOCK=[soft:]hard
```
Maximum locked-in-memory size. Set to infinity for apps like Elasticsearch.

```systemd
MemoryMax=bytes
```
Hard cgroup memory limit. Service is OOM-killed if exceeded. Supports K, M, G, T suffixes.

```systemd
MemoryHigh=bytes
```
Soft cgroup memory limit. Processes throttled (swapped) but not killed.

```systemd
CPUQuota=percentage
```
CPU time limit. 100% = 1 CPU core. 200% = 2 cores.

```systemd
TasksMax=number|infinity
```
Maximum tasks (threads + processes). Default: 4915 (15% of kernel limit).

```systemd
NoNewPrivileges=yes|no
```
Prevent privilege escalation via setuid/setgid binaries. Always enable for untrusted services.

```systemd
ProtectSystem=yes|full|strict
```
Filesystem protection. yes: /usr,/boot readonly. full: + /etc readonly. strict: everything readonly (use ReadWritePaths= for exceptions).

```systemd
ProtectHome=yes|read-only|tmpfs
```
Protect home directories. yes: inaccessible. tmpfs: empty tmpfs mounted over /home.

```systemd
PrivateTmp=yes|no
```
Private /tmp and /var/tmp. Prevents temp file race conditions between services.

```systemd
PrivateDevices=yes|no
```
Private /dev without physical devices. Only pseudo-devices like /dev/null, /dev/zero.

```systemd
ProtectKernelTunables=yes|no
```
Make /proc/sys, /sys readonly. Prevents kernel parameter modification.

```systemd
ProtectKernelModules=yes|no
```
Deny loading kernel modules.

```systemd
ProtectKernelLogs=yes|no
```
Deny access to kernel log ring buffer (/dev/kmsg).

```systemd
ProtectControlGroups=yes|no
```
Make cgroup hierarchy readonly.

```systemd
ProtectHostname=yes|no
```
Prevent changing system hostname.

```systemd
ProtectClock=yes|no
```
Prevent changing system clock.

```systemd
ReadWritePaths=path [path ...]
```
Whitelist writable paths when using ProtectSystem=strict.

```systemd
ReadOnlyPaths=path [path ...]
```
Make additional paths read-only.

```systemd
InaccessiblePaths=path [path ...]
```
Make paths completely inaccessible.

```systemd
CapabilityBoundingSet=cap [cap ...]
```
Restrict Linux capabilities. Empty = no capabilities. Common: CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE (ports < 1024).

```systemd
AmbientCapabilities=cap [cap ...]
```
Capabilities given to unprivileged processes. Allows binding port 80 without root.

```systemd
SystemCallFilter=[@set|syscall] ...
```
Restrict system calls. Sets: @system-service, @basic-io, @file-system, @network-io, @process, @signal.

```systemd
SystemCallArchitectures=native|x86|x86-64|...
```
Restrict syscall ABIs. 'native' = only the host architecture.

```systemd
RestrictNamespaces=yes|no|cgroup ipc net mnt pid user uts
```
Restrict namespace creation. yes = deny all. Or specify allowed types.

```systemd
RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_UNIX ...
```
Restrict socket address families. Limits network access by protocol.

```systemd
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=yes|no
```
Deny memory mappings that are both writable and executable. Prevents JIT and code injection.

```systemd
LockPersonality=yes|no
```
Lock the execution personality (prevents changing ABI).

```systemd
WantedBy=target [target ...]
```
Target that pulls this unit via 'systemctl enable'. multi-user.target = boot. timers.target = timer units.

```systemd
RequiredBy=target [target ...]
```
Hard requirement from target. Rarely used — prefer WantedBy.

```systemd
Also=unit [unit ...]
```
Additional units to enable/disable together with this unit.

```systemd
OnCalendar=expression
```
Calendar event expression. Format: DayOfWeek Year-Month-Day Hour:Min:Sec. Shortcuts: minutely, hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, yearly.

```systemd
OnBootSec=time
```
Trigger timer this long after boot.

```systemd
OnUnitActiveSec=time
```
Trigger this long after the associated unit was last activated. Creates repeating timer.

```systemd
OnUnitInactiveSec=time
```
Trigger this long after the associated unit was last deactivated.

```systemd
Persistent=yes|no
```
If timer missed while system was off, trigger immediately on next boot.

```systemd
RandomizedDelaySec=time
```
Add random delay to prevent multiple timers firing simultaneously (thundering herd).

```systemd
AccuracySec=time
```
Timer accuracy. Lower = more precise, higher = more battery-friendly. Default: 1min.

```systemd
%n
```
Full unit name (e.g., myapp@8080.service).

```systemd
%N
```
Unescaped full unit name.

```systemd
%p
```
Prefix name (before @ for template instances).

```systemd
%i
```
Instance name (between @ and suffix). E.g., 8080 in myapp@8080.service.

```systemd
%I
```
Unescaped instance name.

```systemd
%H
```
Machine hostname.

```systemd
$MAINPID
```
PID of the main process. Available in ExecReload=, ExecStop=.
